summary: Gruber examines Walt Disney's 1957 and 1967 corporate strategy charts, tracing how the company's center of gravity shifted from theatrical films toward theme parks. The 1967 chart, created the year after Walt Disney's death, shows Disneyland rising to near-equal centrality with the film studio, while Disney World Florida appears as a nascent project. Gruber notes the emergence of WED Enterprises (Imagineering) as a major division by 1967, drawing a parallel to Apple's philosophy of owning core technologies. He also surfaces curious historical footnotes, including a failed Disney ski resort and the forgotten cultural role of newspaper comic strips.
key_points: Disney's strategic center shifted from theatrical films in 1957 to theme parks by 1967, a trend that would only accelerate
WED Enterprises (Imagineering) went from absent in the 1957 chart to a major division by 1967, reflecting Disney's evolution into a serious engineering company
The parallel between Disney's Imagineering and Apple's Cook Doctrine — both companies believing they must own and control the primary technologies behind their products
Pre-TV newspaper comic strips served the same cultural role as social media today: free daily engagement through small entertainment nuggets
Disney's go.com domain is a bizarre vestige of the 1990s portal craze, still hosting major Disney properties despite the portal being abandoned in 2001
money_quotes: At the center of the Disney business in the 1957 chart were theatrical films. Everything else — TV, music, magazines and comics, and the then-new Disneyland — funneled into and revolved around the theatrical films.
The trend is obvious: theme parks (including, eventually, floating ones) would replace movies as the core of the company.
Disney was, and should remain, a serious engineering company.
Pre-TV, a daily newspaper comic strip filled the role that social media does today: a free way to engage everyone with small nuggets of entertainment.
The best way to predict the future is to invent it.
We believe that we need to own and control the primary technologies behind the products we make.
key_insight: Disney's strategic evolution from a film studio to an engineering-driven theme park company mirrors the philosophy Apple follows today — that owning and controlling your core technologies is what defines a great company.
